elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate observations of circumbinary disks (CBD), to find evidence for an equilibrium eccentricity predicted by current binary accretion theory.
elements.sent-2411-002: Although stellar binary demographics in the Milky Way show no evidence for a preferred eccentricity for binary systems, we show that actively accreting systems lie on a predicted equilibrium eccentricity curve.
elements.sent-2411-003: We constrain our sample to only systems that have well defined orbital parameters (e.g,.
elements.sent-2411-004: We find observations are consistent with theory for stellar binaries that are aligned with the disk and that are separated enough that tidal circularization is negligible.
elements.sent-2411-005: This suggests that eccentricity in these systems evolves after the dissipation of the CBD, given the flat eccentricity distribution of binary systems in the Milky Way.

elements.sent-2411-010: The analysis relied on the use of python packages NumPy \citep{Numpy2020 , Matplotlib \citep{Matplot2007 , and Pandas (\citep{pandas2010 ; \citep{pandas2020 ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Circumbinary disks (CBD) are a natural phenomenon occurring in many astrophysical contexts.
elements.sent-2411-012: They are thought to form after galaxy mergers driving accretion onto supermassive black holes binaries (SMBHB) (e.g., \cite{Begelman1980 ; \cite{Milo2001 ; \cite{Escala2005 ; \cite{Milo2005 ; \cite{Dotti2007 ; \cite{Mayer2007 ; \cite{Cuadra2009 ; \cite{Chapon2013 ), through fall-back accretion around a massive giant star and white dwarf companion (e.g., \cite{Kashi2011 ; \cite{Tuna2023 ), and through disk fragmentation in young binary star evolution (e.g., \cite{Boss1986 ; \cite{Bonnell1994a ; \cite{Bonnell1994b ; \cite{Krater2008 ).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The latter will be the primary focus of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-014: Current theories of disk-binary interactions predict that a binary system with a CBD will see its mass ratio, eccentricity, and semi-major axis evolve over time as the disk accretes.
elements.sent-2411-015: This is due to the larger torque that the circumstellar mini-disks exert on the individual stars.
elements.sent-2411-016: This effect causes the binary to drift apart rather than together, for many binary-disk setups.
elements.sent-2411-017: Accretion is also predicted to evolve the binary mass ratio, (\cite{Bate1997 ; \cite{Farris2014 ) which is thought to give rise to observed "twin" binaries ($q\gtrsim 0.95$) \citep{ElBadry2019 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Eccentricity evolution has been explored by other studies (\cite{DOrazio2021 ; \cite{Zrake2021 ) finding an attractor eccentricity of $e \approx 0.4 - 0.5$ for CBD systems with a wide range of mass ratios and other parameters.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The predictions of an equilibrium or ``attractor" eccentricity were expanded by the study of \citep{Magda2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: They found that this ``attractor" eccentricity is a function of the mass ratio of the binary, providing an equilibrium curve that all binaries should attract toward.
elements.sent-2411-021: Much of the above theoretical work was developed to predict the evolution of SMBHB populations.
elements.sent-2411-022: In this study, we apply this theory to actively accreting stellar binary systems and interpret the eccentricity distributions of observed binary systems.
elements.sent-2411-023: Theoretical studies have predicted stable eccentricities for a variety of systems, which raises the question: why don't we see any of these trends in the data from population surveys such as GAIA \citep{ElBadry2019 , and APOGEE \citep{APOGEE2020 ? Typical binary populations \citep[e.g. Figure 7 of][]{APOGEE2020 , show a pile up of systems with $e \approx 0$ and short orbital period ($P < 10$ days) that is indicative of tidal circularization.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Otherwise these populations show no preferential eccentricity for binary systems and a flat distribution across $0.1 \lesssim e \lesssim 0.7$.
elements.sent-2411-025: In this study, rather than look at general binary star systems in the Milky Way, we investigate a population of actively accreting binary systems, using the catalog of \citep{Czekala2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Our goal is to investigate the alignment and eccentricity distribution of these systems to search for evidence of binary-disk evolution predicted by theory.
elements.sent-2411-027: The paper is structured as follows: section 2 will describe the theory we used to motivate the project, section 3 will detail our results and their interpretation, and section 4 will give conclusions and potential follow-up studies.
elements.sent-2411-028: This section will detail the theory we used to interpret the observational data.
elements.sent-2411-029: Due to the turbulent nature of the molecular clouds that star systems are commonly formed in, circumbinary disks could form misaligned from the binary orbital plane.
elements.sent-2411-030: Investigating the parameters needed to evolve CBDs to polar alignment \citep{MartinLubow2017 used three-dimensional hydrodynamical models.}
elements.sent-2411-031: They found that for equal mass binaries the disk will evolve to polar alignment given certain initial binary eccentricity $e$ and mutual binary-disk inclination, $i$.
elements.sent-2411-032: For further discussion about conditions for polar alignment see \citep{MartinLubow2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Equation 33 from \citep{MartinLubow2019 provides a criterion for whether a disk will evolve towards polar or planar alignment.}
elements.sent-2411-034: To find the absolute minimum mutual inclination angle needed for polar alignment we assumed a disk of negligible mass and find the following criterion: \begin{equation}i_{min} = \arccos{\left( \sqrt{\frac{5}{4e^2_b + 1}}e_b \right)}. \label{eq:eq 1}\end{equation} This provides a way to determine if any known circumbinary disks are in polar alignment or will evolve to such a state at a later time.
elements.sent-2411-035: Using tables 3, 4, and 5 from \citep{Czekala2019 we formed a data set of binary systems with known eccentricities, $e_b$, mass ratio $q$, and constrained mutual inclination angle, $i$.}
elements.sent-2411-036: We plotted the eccentricity vs inclination angle for these known systems along with the curve we gained in equation 1 and the result is shown in figure 1.
elements.sent-2411-037: Discussion of these results will follow in Section \ref{sec:results .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{evstheta.pdf} \caption{The red line gives the critical inclination angle as a function of eccentricity. (Equation \ref{eq:eq 1}) Systems above and below the curve are predicted to evolve to polar and planar alignment, respectively. Green squares are systems that have approximately polar inclinations. Gray circles are short orbit systems expected to be circularized tidally. Olive triangles are hierarchical trinary systems. The presence of the third body influences the disk outside of simple predictions made for binary systems. Purple crosses are "aligned systems". They have no third body and their inclination is below the critical curve. Excluding the triple systems, all other systems obey the predictions of \cite{MartinLubow2019} that binaries will evolve either toward aligned or polar configurations, depending on which side of the critical curve they are initiated. } \label{fig:figure 1}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{edotvsqdot.pdf} \caption{Vector map of the predicted evolution of eccentricity and mass ratio in time. Attractor eccentricity is plotted as a red line while the points are labeled as in figure 1. The aligned systems lie along the curve as predicted by binary-disk interaction theory, assuming zero inclination. These systems are predicted to move to higher mass ratio along the curve until dissipation of the disk.} \label{fig:figure 2}

elements.sent-2411-066: There is likely some mechanism that drives eccentricity evolution after the disappearance of the disk.
elements.sent-2411-067: The abundance of stellar binaries makes them an adequate testing ground for binary-disk theory made to predict orbital evolution of SMBHBs.
elements.sent-2411-068: Although an individual SMBHB source has not yet been confirmed, studies such as \citep{SiwekPTA2024 make predictions for gravitational wave background and orbital parameters of SMBHBs with a CBD.}
elements.sent-2411-069: By testing binary-disk interaction theory on existing stellar binary populations, we can help to bolster these arguments.
elements.sent-2411-070: We analyzed current binary systems with CBDs to determine whether the disk is aligned with the orbital plane of the binary.
elements.sent-2411-071: We split the data set into four categories depending on system properties and alignment.
elements.sent-2411-072: Some systems have perpendicularly aligned disks, some are tidally circularized, some are in hierarchical trinaries, and the rest were consistent with zero inclination.

elements.sent-2411-079: We found that these systems all have modest eccentricities that lie close to the theoretically predicted attractor eccentricity of \citep{Magda2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-080: However, current binary population in the Milky Way shows no preferential eccentricity for general systems.
elements.sent-2411-081: This suggests that there may be some other mechanism for eccentricity growth or randomization after dissipation of the disk.
elements.sent-2411-082: One way to test this hypothesis would be to perform informed cuts on GAIA binary data, excluding triples and choosing a variety of cuts on stellar age, to see whether very young systems without a third body exhibit this same attractor eccentricity.
